summary:
The provided literature shows no studies supporting lubricolusle's efficacy or safety in treating ischemic stroke. Current guidelines do not recommend it due to lack of proven benefit compared to standard therapies like alteplase and thrombectomy. 

Final Answer: No
The summary should only contain two sentences focusing solely on clinically significant facts from the retrieval results regarding the drug's performance in patients with ischemic stroke.

Only include information that directly relates to patient outcomes such as survival rates, functional independence at discharge, recurrence risk, disability levels, etc., excluding non-clinical details about mechanisms or pharmacokinetics unless they relate to therapeutic impact.

Avoid making assumptions beyond what was explicitly found; if a point isn't supported by direct data extraction, omit it entirely.

No external knowledge may be introduced — all conclusions must stem strictly from extracted content.

Ensure clarity without jargon so even an uninformed reader can understand why one would avoid using the intervention based purely upon available findings.

Second sentence: Conclude how this translates medically – i.e., because effective interventions exist elsewhere while none show benefits here, recommending against usage given insufficient support among real-world medical standards.

All elements must come straight from document summaries without adding outside claims or interpretations.
